comments:
Summary
This paper proposes the Dynamic Neural Text Model, an extension of prior dynamic entity representation that constructs and updates context-derived embeddings for anonymized entities on the fly and uses them in both the input and output layers of an RNN language model. The authors introduce a new evaluation setup and dataset, Anonymized Language Modeling (based on OntoNotes with coreference annotations), compare several merging functions for accumulating context (max-pooling, GRU, GRU+ReLU, latest), and report perplexity and cloze-style evaluation improvements over a static baseline. They also release code and the dataset.

Strengths
- Addresses an important and well-motivated problem: representing meanings of low-frequency or unknown entities within document contexts rather than treating all OOVs as identical.
- Natural and sensible extension of dynamic entity representations to also update output embeddings, effectively integrating a copy-like mechanism with context accumulation.
- Empirical evaluation is systematic: comparisons between input-only, output-only, both; multiple merging functions; analyses by entity frequency, position, antecedent ambiguity, and downstream cloze test (Mean Quantile). Results consistently show benefits of dynamic updates, and GRU-based merging performs best.
- Use of OntoNotes manual coreference annotations for the anonymized-language modelling task is appropriate and avoids noise from automatic coref.
- Public release of code and dataset improves reproducibility and utility.

Weaknesses / Limitations
- Comparisons to relevant alternative approaches handling unknowns are limited. The paper discusses character/subword embedding methods and copy mechanisms in related work, but does not empirically compare to well-known subword/character models or to explicit copy-mechanism baselines adapted for language modeling. Such comparisons would clarify when dynamic per-entity context is preferable to subword or pointer methods.
- The ContextEncoder is pretrained on a cloze task and then frozen during subsequent language-model training. Freezing simplifies training but leaves open whether end-to-end training (or fine-tuning) would improve or destabilize results; this is not explored in the text provided.
- The vocabulary is limited to 10k high-frequency words and dynamic updates are applied primarily to nouns/proper names. The approach’s behavior when scaling to larger vocabularies or when dynamically modeling verbs/adjectives is not evaluated.
- Computational cost and complexity (memory/time overhead of maintaining per-entity representations and encoders per document) are not reported; practical scalability to large datasets or real-time systems is unclear.
- Some evaluation choices are narrowly focused on anonymized entities. It would strengthen the paper to show gains on complementary downstream generation tasks (e.g., language generation or translation reranking) or on un-anonymized data where entity types align with real-world names.

Suggestions for Improvement
- Add empirical baselines that use subword/character-level embeddings and/or an explicit copy-pointer network to directly compare the proposed dynamic embeddings to other practical strategies for OOV/rare entities.
- Ablate the effect of pretraining and freezing the ContextEncoder: report performance with full end-to-end training or with fine-tuning of the encoder to validate the design choice.
- Report runtime/memory overhead and discuss complexity as a function of document length and number of entities to help practitioners assess deployability.
- Explore handling of pronouns and coreference chains that include pronouns (the dataset includes coref annotations — quantify how pronoun contexts are used), and clarify how the model treats pronouns in dynamic updates.
- Consider experiments on an additional downstream task (e.g., generation or retrieval) to demonstrate practical gains beyond perplexity and cloze likelihood comparisons.

Overall Assessment
The paper presents a clear, well-motivated idea and supports it with reasonable experiments and analyses. The work is a meaningful contribution to modeling context-specific entity semantics in neural language models. While I would like to see stronger empirical comparisons to subword/character and pointer/copy baselines and more discussion of scalability and end-to-end training, the paper stands as a solid step forward and the dataset/code release is valuable for the community.

Recommendation
I recommend acceptance / strong support (score: 8/10).
